entries.title: Burp's browser: a built-in Chromium for testing
entries.body:
# Burp's browser - the built-in browser for testing

Burp Suite ships with its own browser - **Burp's browser**. It is a full-fledged Chromium, preconfigured to work through Burp's proxy and with the CA certificate already trusted. The idea is simple: instead of fiddling with configuring the system browser, installing the certificate by hand and fixing TLS errors, you press one button and immediately get a working "browser + Burp" combo.

For a beginner this is the best way to start: zero configuration, HTTPS works out of the box, all traffic immediately ends up in **Proxy > HTTP history**.

## How to launch it

Open the **Proxy > Intercept** tab and click **Open browser**. A Chromium window will open, already wrapped in Burp's proxy. You can immediately type the target's address and browse the application - every request and response will be visible in Burp.

```
Proxy -> Intercept -> [Open browser]
```

## Why it is needed (advantages)

- **Zero configuration.** All proxy-listener parameters are already tuned for this browser. You do not have to dig into system settings, set up FoxyProxy or edit launch flags.
- **HTTPS right away.** The browser trusts the **Burp CA** by default, so HTTPS sites open without warnings about an insecure certificate. That very "download cacert.der and install it" step is simply absent here.
- **Auto-proxying of the scope.** All in-scope traffic automatically goes through Burp as you wander the site - that is, all manual tools (interception, history, sending to **Repeater/Intruder**) work without extra movements.
- **A clean profile.** This is a separate browser without your extensions, accounts and history. On the target it does not drag along extra background (extension syncs, logged-in services), and the traffic comes out cleaner.
- **Passive analysis on the go.** Burp's default live tasks passively crawl and audit the locations you visit: the **Site map** fills up on its own, and potential problems are highlighted without launching a separate scan.
- **A single workflow.** Interception in **Proxy**, analysis in **HTTP history**, sending to **Repeater** and **Intruder** - all of this works with the built-in browser's traffic exactly as you expect, and without surprises in routing.

## When an external browser is better

The built-in browser is good almost always, but sometimes you need your own system Firefox/Chrome:

- you need a specific profile - with logged-in accounts, passwords, bookmarks;
- you need extensions (Wappalyzer, Hackbar, a cookie manager, etc.);
- you are testing behavior tied to a specific browser build/version.

In these cases you point the external browser at `127.0.0.1:8080` and install the **Burp CA** separately (see the section about the certificate in Proxy). For other tasks **Open browser** is faster and more reliable.

## Problems and how to solve them

- **The browser does not open / crashes right after launch.** Burp has a built-in diagnostic tool - **Help > Burp's browser health check**. It runs checks and suggests what is wrong (an incompatible version, lack of permissions, sandbox problems).
- **Launching as root on Linux.** Chromium refuses to start as root with the sandbox enabled. Burp usually offers to add the `--no-sandbox` flag itself. Keep in mind that this reduces the browser's isolation - it is better not to work as root at all, but to run Burp under an ordinary user.
- **Heavy/sluggish on a weak machine.** Chromium is memory-hungry; on a small amount of RAM the built-in browser plus an active scan can noticeably lag. Close unneeded tasks, narrow the scope.
- **Cookies/sessions are not preserved between launches.** This is a separate temporary profile; by default it is not meant to be your permanent browser. If you need a persistent session - that is an argument in favor of an external browser with its own profile.
- **A corporate upstream proxy/VPN.** If internet access is only through a corporate proxy, it must be specified in Burp's settings (upstream proxy), otherwise the built-in browser will not reach external sites.

## Settings

The behavior of the built-in browser is configured in Burp's general settings (the **Settings** section, search by the word `browser`): you can set additional Chromium launch flags, allow running as root, control whether to open a new window each time. There is usually no need to change anything there - the defaults are designed so that everything works right away.

## A minimal workflow

```
1. Proxy -> Intercept -> Open browser
2. You go to the target, log in, click like an ordinary user
3. You look at what accumulated in Proxy -> HTTP history
4. An interesting request -> right click -> Send to Repeater / Send to Intruder
5. You enable Intercept pointwise, when you need to catch and modify a specific request
```

> Tip: for 90% of web tasks the combo of **Open browser + HTTP history + Repeater** covers everything. Do not overcomplicate the external browser setup until you really need extensions or a specific profile.

> Pitfall: the built-in browser solves the problem of trusting the Burp CA only for itself. Mobile apps, thick desktop clients and targets with **certificate pinning** it does not save - there you still need separate fiddling (see Invisible proxying and the section about the certificate).
